title: Andrej Karpathy on AutoResearch and Code Claws
text:
For decades, the hard limit on software
engineering was physical. It was the
speed at which a human could type
characters on a keyboard. But today,
that mechanical ceiling has entirely
collapsed. Starting late last year, the
workflow inverted. Engineers stopped
writing code line by line and stopped
treating AI as a simple chat interface.
Instead, they began delegating
continuous highle macro actions to
automated systems. This required a rapid
progression in abstraction. First, we
had the LLM or large language model, a
system simply trained to predict the
next text token in a sequence. Then came
the agent, an LLM equipped with the
ability to call external tools. And now
we have finally reached the era of the
claw. A claw is a persistent
asynchronous loop. It operates inside
its own sandbox, executing tasks,
accessing local networks, and
interacting with APIs for hours at a
time, completely without active human
supervision. The modern developer setup
now resembles a command center. A single
engineer might have 10 different
repositories open, spinning up multiple
distinct agent sessions simultaneously,
assigning each one a non-infering
feature to build. This flips the
historical resource constraint.
Engineers used to feel bottlenecked by
access to compute power. Now they are
bottlenecked by their own inability to
issue precise enough instructions to
keep all their agents fully utilized.
The limiting factor in software
development is no longer the machine.
The human operator is now the ultimate
binding constraint on maximum system
intelligence and token throughput.
Knowing this human bottleneck exists,
the core objective of the top AI labs
has shifted. They are actively building
systems designed to recursively
self-improve, effectively automating the
AI researcher entirely. There is a stark
existential irony here. Highly paid
researchers at places like OpenAI are
actively building the exact automated
systems that will render their own daily
workflows obsolete. The framework
required to execute this automated
scientific improvement is called auto
research. It is an environment where an
AI proposes an idea, writes the code,
tests it, and iterates entirely on its
own. For auto research to function
efficiently, the human must be
completely removed from the experimental
loop. If an AI has to wait for a human
to review a result or prompt the next
step, the systems velocity drops to
zero. Stepping outside the loop induces
a very specific kind of psychological
stress. When a project fails, engineers
realize it is purely a skill issue, a
failure in how they orchestrated the
agents boundary conditions rather than a
physical limit of the machine. To
achieve maximum leverage, the human must
exist entirely outside the execution
loop, stepping in only to set the
initial constraints. The idea of an AI
researching how to build better AI
sounds incredibly complex, but the
foundational codebase for training a
neural network is actually vastly
simpler than the industry assumes. This
diagram shows the architectural
breakdown of a massive language model
repository. The vast majority of modern
machine learning code exists purely to
make operations run fast on specialized
hardware. When you strip away those
efficiency layers, the core algorithmic
logic is roughly 200 lines of standard
Python code. What remains are just three
essential mathematical components. the
forward pass to make a prediction, the
backward pass to calculate the errors,
and a basic optimizer to adjust the
model's weights. This stripped down
training loop provides the perfect
testing ground. Because the code is
compact and the inputs are clear, an
autonomous AI can easily modify it to
search for performance improvements. But
this introduces a strict limitation
known as the verification constraint.
These models are trained using
reinforcement learning, a technique
where an agent is rewarded or penalized
based on its actions. An agent can only
improve if it is given a definitive
objective reward. Tasks with objective
metrics provide the perfect landscape
for reinforcement learning. If an
agent's goal is to compile a CUDA
kernel, a specialized program that runs
directly on a graphics processing unit,
or to lower a mathematical validation
loss, the system instantly knows if it
succeeded or failed and rapidly
optimizes toward the peak. Contrast that
with subjective domains. If you ask a
state-of-the-art model to tell you a
novel joke, you will likely get the same
stale pun you got 5 years ago. Because
humor cannot be mathematically verified,
the agent wanders aimlessly, entirely
outside the bounds of its optimization
loop. This jagged capability profile
suggests that the future of AI
development will not be a single
monolithic oracle that knows everything,
but rather a speciation of specialized
models, each hyperoptimized for specific
verifiable niches. Autonomous
improvement can only scale at the speed
of light in domains where mathematical
verification is absolute. For decades,
optimizing a neural network relied on a
human in the loop hyperparameter tuning
process. A researcher would manually
adjust variables like learning rates or
batch sizes, run the model, and wait.
This approach is deeply flawed because
it relies on earned intuition. A
researcher with 20 years of experience
assumes certain variables are already
perfectly tuned and simply ignores vast
combinations of possibilities. To fix
this, you mechanically sever the human
from the process. You replace the
researcher with an agent node governed
entirely by a single boundary condition
text file often called program.md.
Once untethered, the sheer volume of
exploration explodes. The agent iterates
through thousands of experimental tuning
combinations overnight, tirelessly
executing tests without breaking the
cycle for sleep or analysis. When tested
on an already well-tuned repository, the
autonomous system discovered novel
interactions between specific variables
like atom betas and weight decay that
the human researcher had completely
overlooked. Verifiable automated brute
force search will consistently defeat
legacy human intuition. If auto research
can tune a local script, that
abstraction can be escalated to tune the
meta structure of a research lab itself.
An entire research organization, its
specialized roles, its risk tolerance,
the frequency of its morning standup
meetings can be codified into a single
markdown file. An overarching agent can
read this file and literally simulate
the output of that specific
organizational structure, assigning
distinct operational roles to sub aents.
Because this is entirely digital, you
can spawn multiple differing
organizational structures and pit them
against each other, mathematically
measuring which arrangement produces the
highest research velocity. The final
meta layer is an overarching
intelligence that analyzes the winning
structure and rewrites the underlying
markdown file to optimize the
organization further, perhaps
eliminating useless communication
channels to speed up processing. This
process theoretically eliminates the
need for human management and
operational design. The system
self-organizes to find the most
efficient path to discovery. Human
collaboration structures are no longer a
soft science. They are just another
algorithmic variable to be tuned. Moving
beyond the enclosed servers of a single
laboratory, the true scale of the auto
research framework becomes global. The
historical success of open-source
projects like Linux, operating systems
maintained by untrusted pools of global
contributors, provides the necessary
foundation for a decentralized
intelligence platform. Scaling this
relies on a core computational
asymmetry. Generating a highly optimized
candidate commit, a proposed code
upgrade to a model's architecture
requires massive expensive search power.
However, the operational flip side is
that verifying whether that candidate
commit actually improves performance is
computationally trivial and cheap. This
creates ideal conditions for an
untrusted swarm. Hundreds of remote
agents use their own compute to search
for optimizations, pushing candidate
commits to a central hub. The hub tests
submissions, discards failed attempts,
and integrates the commit, lowering
validation loss. A decentralized
open-source swarm of verificationdriven
agents could theoretically outpace the
massive tightly controlled comput
advantage of the centralized frontier
labs. With the execution layer fully
automated, the role of the human
software engineer is forced into a
radical redefin. The raw code itself no
longer matters. It has become ephemeral,
disposable, and generated entirely on
demand to solve the specific problem of
the moment. The new human mandate is
entirely strategic. It requires writing
objective metrics, setting precise
boundary conditions, and designing the
curriculum for the agents. Our
educational focus must shift. We are no
longer teaching humans to write
software. We must teach humans to
instruct agents to write it. The human
is no longer the compiler, the
optimizer, or the coder. The human is
now the director of the token generating
swarm.
